paragraphs.text: As the umbrella organisation of consumer organisations in the field of consumer protection, the Union of Slovakia welcomes the initiative of the European Commission and considers the proposal for a Digital Justice Act as a key step towards aligning the digital market with the principles of trust, equality and accountability. Based on the experience of our member organisations, we highlight the following fundamental facts: 1. Weak enforcement of the current rules – Consumer law in the digital space suffers from non-systematic interpretation and a low level of enforcement. Many unfair practices (e.g. fake discounts, misleading charges, difficult cancellation of subscriptions) are formally prohibited, but there is a lack of real and effective enforcement. Supervisory authorities (e.g. in Slovakia) do not always consider digital manipulation practices to be a priority. We therefore support that the envisaged act contains precise definitions of prohibited digital techniques, puts in place tools for uniform enforcement across the EU, allows for swift reporting and interim measures, including across borders. 2. Urgent need for collective instruments – Collective actions exist only on paper without any support from the state. The Slovak Republic adopted Act No 261/2023 on collective actions by consumers, transposing Directive (EU) 2020/1828. Thus, formally there is a system in place, but it is in practice dysfunctional. To date, no known collective action has been brought under that law. The causes are systemic: The law is procedurally complex and formally overburdens the applicant with evidence and formalities prior to the proceedings. There is no financial or organisational support to qualified entities, no subsidies, no procedural advantages, no standardised tools to enforce consumer rights. State authorities do not actively participate in the system. Judicial practice is unclear and reticent claimants face legal uncertainty. As a result, even if the interested parties are registered in the proceedings, the whole system presents a considerable risk and a minimum chance of success. Without active support from public institutions, this is a formal, not a realistic option. For this reason, we recommend that the Digital Justice Act: it introduced an obligation for Member States to support qualified entities materially and professionally, provided for the possibility of cross-border action coordinated at EU level, and that any new legislation on consumer law automatically presupposes collective enforceability. Without these measures, consumer protection in the digital environment will remain declaratory and irrecoverable. 3. Lack of harmonisation between digital and consumer legislation – Even when the DSA, DMA, AI Act or the Audiovisual Services Directive were adopted, there are significant gaps in application between regulations: protection against personalisation of prices is fragmented, platform or trader definitions differ, the right to simple cancellation of a contract is not standardised across services. We therefore call for the Digital Justice Act to be systematically aligned with existing legal frameworks and to eliminate legislative inconsistencies that hamper effective protection. 4. The crucial importance of protecting vulnerable groups – Vulnerable consumers, children, seniors, people with disabilities are most at risk from digital practices. Their ability to critically evaluate manipulative elements is limited and the consequences are severe (psychological, financial, health). For this reason, we ask to include in the proposal: prohibition of personalisation of vulnerability-based advertising, right to human intervention in any interaction with AI or chatbots, guarantee of access to alternative (non-digital) modes of communication with services. We conclude that only legislation that goes beyond principles and focuses on tools, enforceability and equality of access can bring real fairness to the digital environment.
